Sherwin Dai
Introduction Philosophy Advocate Cultural Leader Researcher

Sherwin Dai

Husky Leadership Certificate ePortfolio

"Every person deserves the chance to realize their potential."

University of Washington · Class of 2026

Personal Introduction

Get to Know Me

Hello! My name is Sherwin and I am a senior at the University of Washington majoring in Psychology with a minor in Data Science. I am a Resident Advisor at Alder Hall, a Research Assistant in the Language, Cognition and Development Lab, and a four-year team leader with UW Riverways Education Partnerships. I am also bilingual in English and Chinese with conversational Japanese, and I love gaming, figure skating, and exploring Japanese culture. I am excited to share my leadership journey with you! First, let me tell you about the values that guide my work: curiosity, compassion, growth, equity, and connection.

Core Values

Curiosity

Curiosity is my drive to understand how and why things work — whether that is how children develop mathematical thinking or how a student in a rural community experiences STEM for the first time. It is the foundation of everything I do. Curiosity pushes me to look beyond surface-level answers and to always dig deeper, which I believe is essential both in research and in connecting with people.

Compassion

Compassion means genuinely caring about the experiences of others and being willing to act on that care. It is not just feeling empathy but translating it into action — supporting a resident going through a tough time, mentoring a student who feels like college is out of reach, or simply listening when someone needs it. Leadership without care is hollow, and the moments I have had the greatest impact have been when I set aside my own agenda and truly listened.

Growth

Growth means being committed to continuous learning and honest self-reflection, even when it is uncomfortable. I have seen how much I have changed since my freshman year — from a quiet participant on my first Riverways trip to leading a team of six and teaching 70 students. Growth reminds me that I am not a finished product and that the willingness to evolve is what makes a leader effective.

Equity

Equity means recognizing that not everyone starts from the same place and actively working to level the playing field. Teaching STEM in Brewster, an underserved community where sports are prioritized because they feel like the only path to recognition, opened my eyes to how differently opportunity is distributed. Equity drives my passion for making STEM accessible and my future goals of addressing underrepresentation in academic and professional spaces.

Connection

Connection is about building genuine, trust-based relationships and creating a sense of belonging. The most impactful work happens through relationships, not in isolation. As an RA, the residents I have helped the most are the ones I built authentic relationships with. As a Riverways leader, the students who were most engaged were the ones who felt we genuinely cared about them.

Leadership Identities

Advocate Mentor Culturally-Responsive Leader Researcher

Advocate: I use my voice and position to champion the potential of others, especially students in rural and underrepresented communities who do not always have someone in their corner.

Mentor: I lead through one-on-one relationships — guiding residents through college life and investing in people the way others have invested in me.

Culturally-Responsive Leader: I draw on my bilingual and bicultural background to lead in ways that honor people's contexts rather than forcing a one-size-fits-all approach. Teaching in tribal and rural communities through Riverways taught me that effective leadership starts with understanding the cultural strengths and barriers people bring with them.

Researcher: I ground my leadership in evidence through my work in Dr. Starr's lab studying how spatial language relates to children's mathematical development. Research challenges me to think critically about the claims I make and the actions I take.

My "Why"

I believe every person deserves the chance to realize their potential, and I have seen too many people held back not by ability but by access. In Brewster, I watched sixth graders complete their first robot obstacle course and realize they could do something they thought was impossible. That moment changed me. I discovered that I gain deep fulfillment from helping people achieve goals they initially believed were out of reach, and that insight now drives my research, my work as an RA, and my pursuit of graduate school.

Future Goals

Looking ahead, I plan to pursue a graduate degree in cognitive science or cognitive neuroscience with the goal of bridging research and practice to create more equitable learning environments. As an anti-racist leader, I am committed to ensuring my future work actively addresses the systemic barriers that keep underrepresented groups out of STEM. I envision leadership that combines rigorous research with community-centered action — mentoring students from marginalized backgrounds and advocating for practices that distribute opportunity more equitably. Leadership does not stop at graduation, and I cannot wait to see where this journey takes me.

Leadership Philosophy

What Leadership Means to Me

"Leadership, to me, is not a title or a position but a practice."

It is the ongoing process of showing up with intention, listening deeply, and creating space for others to grow alongside you. At its core, leadership means being willing to act on your values even when it is uncomfortable, and to do so in a way that uplifts rather than diminishes the people around you. For leadership to be meaningful, it must also be equitable — it has to account for who is in the room, who has historically been left out, and how one's actions either reinforce or challenge those patterns.

One leadership theory that deeply resonates with me is Servant Leadership. This framework suggests that effective leaders should act as servants first and leaders second, emphasizing a calling to give back rather than pursuing self-serving motives or placing an overemphasis on production. This theory has been influential to my personal perspective because it shifts the focus of leadership from power and status to empathy and support. In my experience, the most effective environments are those where the leader prioritizes the growth and well-being of their team members. By adopting a servant-first mindset, a leader can foster a culture of trust and collaboration that naturally leads to better outcomes. It reminds me that the true measure of leadership is not how many people serve the leader, but how well the leader serves others to help them reach their full potential.

My understanding of leadership has grown significantly through my experiences as a Resident Advisor, a team leader with UW Riverways Education Partnerships, and through the Husky Leadership Certificate program. Early on, I understood leadership mostly as a role — the person in charge directs others. That changed when I became an RA and found myself responsible for a community of peers rather than just completing a task. I learned quickly that leading peers requires a different kind of presence, one built on trust, consistency, and genuine care. Similarly, through four years with Riverways and progressing from participant to team leader, I saw how effective leadership means meeting people where they are. Teaching STEM to students in rural and tribal communities taught me that you cannot lead without first understanding the barriers and strengths that the people you are working with bring to the table. These experiences showed me that servant leadership is not just a theory — it is the approach that has consistently produced the most meaningful outcomes in my own practice.

What has remained constant across all of these experiences is the importance of honest self-reflection. I have found that the moments I grew most as a leader were the moments I was willing to examine my own assumptions and adjust. Being bicultural and multilingual has also deepened my perspective, showing me that leadership looks different across contexts and cultures and that there is no single right way to lead. The most important thing is to stay grounded in your purpose, remain open to the perspectives of others, and never stop working toward something bigger than yourself.

Key Learning Experience 1 · Co-Curricular

STEM Education Leadership at Brewster Middle School

Leadership Identity: Advocate

Overview

During spring break of my sophomore year, I led a team of six UW students teaching 70 sixth graders at Brewster Middle School how to program LEGO robots through the CELE Center's Riverways program. I had gone as a member my freshman year and fell in love with the experience, so I applied and got the leadership position. Over the week, we developed interactive lessons that made programming accessible and fun for students in an underserved, predominantly Hispanic community. We divided students into six groups so each of us could work closely with them, and we even redesigned our obstacle courses overnight when we realized they were too easy. This experience showed me how powerful it can be to help students realize they can achieve things they didn't think were possible.

Personal Importance of Work

This experience connects deeply to why I'm interested in education — helping people achieve something they believe they couldn't achieve. Brewster is very underserved with a large Hispanic population. A lot of students want to go to college but are scared of the cost or not sure if they can make it. The school is very sports focused because they believe that's the only way to win something and get funding, while STEM and other academic activities are often overlooked and seen as not cool. Students face challenges like fewer education resources and family pressure to go to work full time right after high school, which makes them doubt themselves. Hopefully through exposing more students to STEM, they can realize it's not only for "smart" people and that they can do it too if they put in the effort. This connects directly to my graduate school goals of addressing underrepresentation in STEM fields.

Tasks Accomplished

  • Led and coordinated a team of 6 UW students, each with specific roles like photographer, driver, chef, and lesson leader
  • Taught 70 sixth-grade students how to program LEGO robots to navigate obstacle courses
  • Divided students into 6 groups and rotated between them to discuss concepts and gauge their learning
  • Redesigned obstacle courses overnight after the first day to make them more challenging with harder angles and turns
  • Created a hands-on analogy using toys that resemble making a sandwich to help students understand for loops in programming
  • Had conversations with students about college access, financial aid, and UW resources
  • Managed classroom dynamics through interactive lesson plans and fun competitive games between groups

Leadership Competencies

Problem Solving
Learning and Reasoning

Leading the Brewster program required constant problem solving, often under time pressure and without the luxury of planning ahead. The biggest challenge was maintaining engagement with 70 excited sixth graders who quickly lost interest when lessons felt too easy or too abstract. When students couldn't grasp for loops through our initial explanation, we invented a sandwich-making analogy on the spot using physical toys, and suddenly the concept clicked. When our obstacle courses turned out to be too simple after the first day, we stayed up redesigning them with harder angles and turns. The dimension I developed most was proficiency — I built the skill of diagnosing problems in real time and generating workable solutions rather than freezing or defaulting to the original plan. I learned that effective problem solving starts with asking why something isn't working before jumping to a fix. I still need to improve at anticipating problems during the planning phase rather than always reacting in the moment. In future teaching and research roles, I plan to build structured checkpoints into my work so I can catch issues before they escalate.

Helping Others
Interpersonal Interaction

At its core, the Brewster experience was about helping others — not just teaching STEM content, but supporting students in seeing themselves as capable learners. Many of the sixth graders came into the week believing that programming was something only "smart" kids could do, and a big part of my role was challenging that belief through encouragement and scaffolded instruction. I made a point of sitting with groups who were struggling, walking through problems step by step, and celebrating their progress out loud so other students could see that success was possible. The dimension I developed most was performance — I consistently put helping behaviors into practice throughout the week rather than just intending to be supportive. I learned that genuinely helping others requires paying attention to what they actually need, not what I assume they need. I still want to get better at recognizing when my help is becoming too directive rather than empowering. Going forward, I will carry this orientation into my RA work and future mentoring roles, always centering the other person's agency in my support.

Mentoring
Interpersonal Interaction

Beyond classroom instruction, I found myself in informal mentoring conversations with students about their futures. During breaks and lunch, several students asked about college — whether they could afford it, whether they were "smart enough," and what UW was actually like. I shared my own experience honestly, including the financial aid resources that made college possible, and tried to make the idea of higher education feel tangible rather than abstract. I also mentored my team members, checking in daily about how their lessons went and offering feedback on their teaching approaches. The dimension I developed most was significance — I came to deeply value the impact that even brief mentoring conversations can have on someone's trajectory. I learned that mentoring is less about having all the answers and more about listening and affirming someone's potential. I still need to develop more structured approaches to mentoring rather than relying on informal moments. In the future, I plan to seek formal mentoring roles in graduate school and in organizations focused on STEM access.

Others' Circumstances
Civic Responsibility

Working in Brewster opened my eyes to how dramatically different students' circumstances can be from my own. I grew up in Redmond, Washington, where STEM resources were abundant and college was a default expectation. In Brewster, many families depended on agricultural work, and students faced pressure to contribute economically right after high school rather than pursue further education. The school prioritized sports because that was seen as the most viable path to recognition and funding, while STEM activities were overlooked. The dimension I developed most was cognition — I deepened my understanding of how socioeconomic context, geography, and cultural expectations shape educational access in ways that are invisible to those who benefit from privilege. I learned that understanding others' circumstances requires listening to their stories rather than projecting my assumptions. I still need to grow in learning about communities before entering them with a program. Moving forward, I will approach any educational outreach by first seeking to understand the community's existing strengths and needs.

Initiative
Personal Behavior

This experience required me to take initiative at every stage, from applying for the leadership position after my freshman year to making real-time decisions during the program itself. When I noticed that students were disengaged during our initial lessons, I didn't wait for someone else to propose a solution — I gathered my team and initiated the overnight obstacle course redesign. I also took the initiative to have college access conversations with students during unstructured time, recognizing that those moments mattered even though they weren't part of our formal lesson plan. The dimension I developed most was performance — I consistently acted on opportunities rather than waiting for permission or direction. I learned that initiative is not about doing everything yourself but about recognizing when action is needed and mobilizing resources to make it happen. I still want to improve at taking initiative in less urgent situations, where the need for action is less obvious. In graduate school and beyond, I plan to maintain this proactive approach to identifying problems and creating solutions.

Responding to Change
Personal Behavior

Almost nothing during the Brewster trip went exactly as planned, and I had to learn to respond to change productively rather than with frustration. Our obstacle courses needed a complete overhaul after day one. Our teaching methods had to shift when students couldn't grasp abstract programming concepts. The classroom energy was far more chaotic than we anticipated, which required us to rethink our entire approach to engagement. Instead of viewing these changes as failures, I came to see them as information — signals that our approach needed adjustment. The dimension I developed most was efficacy — I built confidence in my own ability to handle unexpected situations without losing momentum. I learned that responding well to change means staying curious about what isn't working instead of becoming defensive about my original plan. I still need to build more flexibility into my initial plans rather than always adapting after the fact. I will apply this skill in my research work, where unexpected results or methodological challenges require the same kind of composed, flexible response.

Confidence
Personal Behavior

Leading a team and teaching 70 students as a sophomore pushed me well beyond my comfort zone and forced me to develop genuine confidence in my abilities. During my freshman year on the trip, I was a participant who followed others' lead. Stepping into the leadership role meant I had to trust my own judgment when making decisions about lesson plans, team coordination, and classroom management. When our first day didn't go as planned, I had to project confidence to my team even while internally feeling uncertain, and that act of leading through uncertainty gradually became real confidence as I saw our adjustments succeed. The dimension I developed most was efficacy — I built a genuine belief in my ability to lead, teach, and adapt in challenging environments. I learned that confidence isn't the absence of doubt but the willingness to act despite it. I still need to work on maintaining confidence when I receive critical feedback, rather than interpreting it as evidence that I'm not ready. Moving forward, I will draw on this experience whenever I face new leadership challenges, remembering that I've led through uncertainty before and can do it again.

Artifact

Figure 1: The UW STEM Alternative Spring Break team before departing for Brewster Middle School. Our team of six led a week of LEGO robotics programming for 70 sixth-grade students in an underserved, predominantly Hispanic community. This photo represents the collaborative spirit and shared purpose that defined the experience — each team member brought unique strengths that made our teaching more effective and our impact more meaningful.

Lessons Learned

This experience really reminded me that being a teacher means learning from your students too. I discovered that I gain a lot of happiness when helping people in need, which made me want to research more into psychology so I can understand how we behave and interact and help more people. The adaptability I developed translates directly to my current research work in the Language, Cognition and Development Lab, where I regularly have to adjust my analytical approaches based on what patterns I'm seeing in the data. The understanding of others' circumstances I started developing helps me think about diversity in research studies and who we're including or excluding. Most importantly, this experience taught me that education is mutual — a lesson I carry into every mentorship opportunity I have now as an RA in Alder Hall. As I apply to graduate programs, I'm committed to doing research that creates actual pathways for underrepresented students to access STEM fields, just like we tried to do for those Brewster sixth graders.

Key Learning Experience 2 · Curricular

Social Psychology

Leadership Identity: Culturally-Responsive Leader

Overview

Social Psychology at the University of Washington offered me a structured lens through which to examine how individuals think, feel, and behave in social contexts. Through regular DIY (Do It Yourself) reflection assignments, I engaged deeply with course material by connecting theoretical concepts to real-world observations and personal experiences. The coursework placed particular emphasis on prejudice and stereotyping — how they form, persist, and cause harm at both individual and systemic levels. This experience was valuable not only because it sharpened my analytical thinking, but because it pushed me to examine my own assumptions and biases in ways that feel directly relevant to my aspirations in cognitive science and organizational psychology.

Personal Importance of Work

My long-term career goal is to address underrepresentation in STEM, particularly through applications in recruitment and organizational design. Social psychology gave me a rigorous academic foundation for understanding why underrepresentation persists — not just structurally, but psychologically. Studying prejudice and stereotyping revealed how implicit biases shape decision-making in hiring, mentorship, and institutional gatekeeping. The DIY assignments made this personal: I had to sit with uncomfortable truths about how social categories influence perception, including my own. As someone who has worked with rural and tribal communities through Riverways, I saw firsthand how stereotyping affects students' access to opportunity. This class gave me the language and theory to understand those experiences more deeply and advocate more effectively.

Tasks Accomplished

  • Completed multiple DIY reflection assignments connecting social psychology theories to personal observations, current events, and professional contexts
  • Analyzed foundational research on prejudice, stereotyping, and discrimination, including landmark studies on implicit bias and intergroup conflict
  • Engaged with course readings and lectures on topics including social cognition, attitude formation, and the psychology of group identity
  • Applied theoretical frameworks (e.g., social identity theory, stereotype threat) to real-world scenarios in written reflections
  • Synthesized course concepts to develop a more critical, evidence-based perspective on systemic inequity

Leadership Competencies

Self-Understanding
Self-Awareness and Development

The prejudice and stereotyping unit was the most personally challenging part of the course — and the most formative. Writing DIY reflections forced me to examine moments in my own life where I had held or internalized stereotypes, even unconsciously. I had to confront the gap between how I saw myself (someone committed to equity) and how social cognition research suggested I actually process social information (through the same biased shortcuts as everyone else). The dimension I developed most was cognition — I deepened my understanding of who I am as a social being, including the uncomfortable parts. I learned that genuine self-understanding requires vulnerability and a willingness to be wrong about yourself. This is not a one-time realization but an ongoing practice of checking my assumptions against evidence. I still need to work on translating self-understanding into consistent behavioral change rather than letting insights remain intellectual. In graduate school and in future organizational roles, I plan to use this capacity for honest self-examination as a foundation for more intentional, equitable decision-making.

Personal Values
Self-Awareness and Development

Social psychology helped me clarify and pressure-test my personal values around equity, honesty, and intellectual humility. Before this course, I would have said I valued fairness — but studying how prejudice operates at the systemic level showed me that fairness as an abstract ideal isn't enough without understanding the mechanisms that produce unfairness. The DIY assignments forced me to articulate not just what I value but why, and to examine whether my actions align with those values. For example, reflecting on stereotype threat research made me realize that valuing inclusion means actively designing environments where people feel they belong, not just avoiding overt exclusion. The dimension I developed most was significance — I came to understand why holding clear personal values matters, particularly for someone who wants to work in diversity and organizational design. I learned that values are only meaningful if they translate into practice. I still want to grow in holding myself accountable when my behavior doesn't match my stated values. Going forward, I plan to revisit my core values regularly and evaluate whether my professional and personal choices reflect them.

Empathy
Interpersonal Interaction

The course material on prejudice and discrimination cultivated a deeper sense of empathy in me — not just emotional sympathy, but the cognitive effort of understanding experiences fundamentally different from my own. Studying how stereotype threat affects academic performance, for instance, helped me empathize with the pressure that students from marginalized groups carry into every test, classroom, and interview. The DIY reflections pushed me to connect these findings to real people in my life: students I worked with in Brewster, residents I support as an RA, and peers navigating identity-related challenges at UW. The dimension I developed most was cognition — I built a more sophisticated understanding of what empathy actually requires, which is not projecting my own feelings onto others but genuinely trying to comprehend their perspective and context. I learned that empathy without structural awareness can be patronizing; it needs to be paired with knowledge of the systems that shape people's experiences. I still want to improve at practicing empathy in high-stress or conflict situations, where my instinct is to become solution-oriented rather than present. In my future work in organizational psychology, I plan to center empathy as a core practice in how I approach research participants, colleagues, and the communities I study.

Advocating for a Point of View
Communication

The DIY assignments required me to do more than summarize research — they demanded that I take a position and defend it with evidence. Each reflection pushed me to formulate a clear argument about how psychological theory applied to real-world contexts, and to anticipate counterarguments or alternative interpretations. For instance, when writing about how implicit bias affects hiring decisions, I had to move beyond stating that bias exists and instead advocate for specific structural interventions, grounding my argument in the research we studied. The dimension I developed most was proficiency — I built the skill of constructing evidence-based arguments and presenting them with clarity and conviction. I learned that effective advocacy is not about being the loudest voice but about being precise, well-supported, and responsive to opposing perspectives. I still need to work on advocating for my views in live discussions with the same confidence I have in writing. In graduate school and in professional contexts, I plan to apply this skill when presenting research findings and when arguing for evidence-based policy changes in organizational settings.

Listening
Communication

While this was a lecture-based course, some of the most impactful learning happened when I practiced genuine listening — both to the course material and to peers during discussions. The prejudice and stereotyping content challenged me to listen without immediately defending or deflecting, especially when topics touched on my own identity or assumptions. During class discussions, I noticed that my instinct was sometimes to formulate a response before someone had finished speaking, rather than fully absorbing their perspective. The DIY reflections helped me practice a more deliberate form of listening: sitting with research findings that challenged my worldview, re-reading passages I initially dismissed, and letting uncomfortable ideas sit with me before responding. The dimension I developed most was cognition — I developed a clearer understanding of what active, non-defensive listening looks like and why it matters, especially in conversations about identity and bias. I learned that listening is not the same as waiting for your turn to speak; it requires genuine curiosity about what the other person is communicating. I still need to work on listening more patiently in emotionally charged conversations. I will bring this practice into my RA work, my research collaborations, and eventually into organizational settings where understanding diverse perspectives is essential.

Integrative Thinking
Custom / Additional Competency

Perhaps the most distinctive feature of this course was its demand for integration — connecting psychological research to sociology, history, policy, and personal experience simultaneously. The DIY format made this explicit: I couldn't just describe a study; I had to locate it within a larger context of meaning. For example, I connected stereotype threat research to my own observations of students in underserved communities during Riverways, and linked social identity theory to dynamics I've observed in residence hall communities as an RA. The dimension I developed most was proficiency — I practiced the skill of drawing connections across disciplinary boundaries and levels of analysis. I learned that integrative thinking has to be deliberately practiced, not assumed; it requires actively searching for links between ideas that seem unrelated on the surface. I still want to improve at integrating quantitative data with qualitative experience more fluidly in my writing. My goal is to continue developing this capacity in graduate work, where interdisciplinary thinking will be essential to studying the intersection of cognition, identity, and organizational behavior.

Artifact

"Before taking this social psychology class, I understood prejudice and gender discrimination primarily in abstract terms — as unfair treatment that should be eliminated — but I didn't have a framework for understanding the psychological mechanisms that create and maintain these attitudes. Analyzing Dangal through the lens of concepts like stereotype threat, hostile versus benevolent sexism, the contact hypothesis, and institutional discrimination has given me a much more nuanced appreciation for both the film's strengths and its limitations. I now recognize that the film accurately depicts many aspects of gender stereotypes and their harmful effects, but it also presents an overly optimistic narrative that individual achievement alone can overcome deeply entrenched prejudice."
Figure 2: An excerpt from my DIY 4 reflection assignment for Social Psychology, in which I analyzed the Indian film Dangal through the lens of prejudice research, stereotype threat, and the contact hypothesis. This assignment exemplifies how the course pushed me to apply theoretical frameworks to real-world media and to critically evaluate both the strengths and limitations of how psychological concepts are portrayed. Writing this reflection deepened my understanding of how prejudice operates at multiple levels and strengthened my ability to advocate for evidence-based perspectives.

Lessons Learned

Social psychology taught me that understanding human behavior requires both intellectual rigor and personal honesty. The experience of writing DIY reflections on prejudice and stereotyping pushed me to hold theory and lived experience in tension — a skill I believe is essential for anyone working toward equity in research or organizational contexts. The competencies I developed here, especially integrative thinking and self-understanding, have already translated into how I approach my RA work and my research in the Starr Lab. As I move into graduate programs in cognitive or organizational psychology, I carry this foundation with me: a commitment to evidence-based thinking, a more honest reckoning with bias, and a clearer sense of why the work I want to do matters.

Key Learning Experience 3 · Co-Curricular

Decoding the Connection Between Words and Numbers

Leadership Identity: Researcher

Overview

As a Research Assistant in Dr. Ariel Starr's Language, Cognition and Development Lab at the University of Washington, I contributed to a study investigating how parents' use of spatial vocabulary during everyday interactions relates to their preschool-aged children's mathematical achievement. My role involved analyzing parent-child speech transcripts using R programming, developing code to extract and categorize linguistic data, and collaborating with a team of researchers to ensure data accuracy and integrity. This experience was invaluable because it allowed me to apply my psychology coursework to real-world developmental research while strengthening my technical and collaborative skills in an academic setting.

Personal Importance of Work

This research deeply connects to my passion for understanding how early learning experiences shape children's cognitive development and to my broader goal of addressing underrepresentation in STEM fields. By studying how spatial language exposure in the home environment relates to math outcomes, our lab contributes to understanding one mechanism through which educational disparities may emerge before children even enter formal schooling. As someone who has spent four years leading STEM education programs for students in rural and tribal communities through UW Riverways, I am acutely aware of how early access to rich learning environments matters. This lab experience reinforced my commitment to pursuing graduate work in cognitive science and using research to inform more equitable educational practices.

Tasks Accomplished

  • Developed and refined R scripts to process Excel files containing parent-child interaction transcripts
  • Created functions for sentence counting, question identification, and spatial vocabulary categorization
  • Cleaned and validated large datasets to ensure accuracy and consistency across transcript files
  • Generated descriptive statistics tables and summaries for academic presentations and publications
  • Participated in weekly lab meetings, presenting progress updates and troubleshooting methodological challenges collaboratively
  • Contributed to the preparation of conference presentation materials summarizing key findings
  • Maintained organized documentation of all coding decisions and data processing steps for lab records

Leadership Competencies

Research
Learning and Reasoning

Through my work in the Language, Cognition and Development Lab, I developed the research competency by engaging directly in the research process from data processing to analysis. I learned to design systematic approaches for extracting meaningful linguistic patterns from raw transcript data, which required understanding both the theoretical framework of our study and the practical demands of working with large datasets. The dimension I developed most was proficiency — I built tangible skills in R programming, data cleaning, and statistical analysis that I did not have before joining the lab. I learned that effective research demands patience and meticulous attention to detail; a single coding error in a script could compromise an entire dataset. I still need to strengthen my ability to independently design research questions and develop study methodologies from the ground up. Moving forward, I plan to carry these research skills into graduate school, where I hope to lead my own studies on how cognitive processes influence STEM learning outcomes.

Analysis
Learning and Reasoning

Working with parent-child speech transcripts required me to develop strong analytical skills, as I had to break down complex linguistic data into meaningful categories and identify patterns across hundreds of interactions. For instance, I created R functions that could distinguish spatial vocabulary from general language use and then cross-reference those findings with children's math assessment scores. The dimension I grew most in was cognition — I deepened my understanding of how to systematically approach data analysis, moving beyond surface-level observations to identify underlying trends. I learned that effective analysis requires both technical precision and conceptual clarity; knowing what to look for is just as important as knowing how to look. One area I want to improve is my ability to interpret unexpected results without immediately assuming errors in my process. In my future work, I intend to apply this analytical mindset to organizational research, examining patterns in workplace practices that affect STEM diversity and inclusion.

Self-Development
Self-Awareness and Development

My time in the lab pushed me to continually develop new skills and seek out growth opportunities. When I first joined, I had only basic familiarity with R, and the demands of the project required me to rapidly teach myself advanced data manipulation and text processing techniques. I spent time outside of lab hours working through tutorials, debugging my code, and asking senior lab members for guidance. The dimension I developed most was motivation — I was internally driven to become more competent because I understood how my contributions directly impacted our research outcomes. I learned that self-development is not a passive process; it requires actively identifying gaps in my knowledge and seeking resources to fill them. I still want to improve my comfort with asking for help earlier rather than struggling alone for extended periods. Going forward, I plan to maintain this growth-oriented approach in graduate school, proactively seeking mentorship and professional development opportunities to strengthen my research capabilities.

Collaboration
Interpersonal Interaction

Research in the lab was inherently collaborative, requiring me to coordinate closely with Dr. Starr and fellow research assistants to maintain consistency in our data processing and analysis. I regularly participated in lab meetings where we discussed our progress, troubleshot coding challenges together, and provided feedback on each other's work. One specific example was when a teammate and I discovered inconsistencies in how we were categorizing certain spatial terms; we worked together to develop a shared coding manual that standardized our approach. The dimension I grew in most was performance — I consistently engaged in collaborative behaviors when situations called for it, rather than defaulting to working independently. I learned that effective collaboration requires clear communication about expectations and a willingness to adapt one's approach based on the team's needs. I still need to work on initiating collaborative conversations earlier in a project rather than waiting until problems arise. In the future, I will apply these collaboration skills in graduate research teams and in professional settings focused on improving STEM recruitment.

Organizational Behavior
Group Dynamics

Being part of the lab gave me firsthand insight into how research teams function as organizations with their own norms, hierarchies, and communication structures. I observed how Dr. Starr facilitated a lab culture that balanced accountability with psychological safety — we were expected to meet deadlines and produce high-quality work, but we were also encouraged to ask questions and admit when we were struggling. I noticed how the onboarding process for new research assistants shaped their integration into the team and how regular lab meetings served as a mechanism for maintaining alignment on shared goals. The dimension I developed most was cognition, as I came to understand how organizational structures and leadership styles directly influence team productivity and morale. I learned that even small groups like a research lab have complex dynamics that require intentional management. I want to continue developing my ability to recognize and respond to group dynamics in real time. I plan to apply this understanding in future roles where I can help create inclusive, well-functioning team environments.

Social Responsibility
Civic Responsibility

Our lab's research on spatial language and mathematical development carries significant social implications, particularly for understanding how early home environments contribute to achievement gaps. Throughout my work, I was continually reminded that our data represented real families and real children, and that our findings could inform interventions aimed at supporting equitable early childhood education. This awareness deepened my sense of social responsibility as a researcher. The dimension I developed most was significance — I came to genuinely value the importance of conducting research that serves the public good, not just academic advancement. I learned that socially responsible research requires thoughtfulness at every stage, from how we recruit participants to how we communicate findings. I still want to grow in my ability to translate research findings into accessible recommendations for practitioners and policymakers. In the future, I aim to conduct research that directly addresses systemic barriers to STEM participation, particularly for communities that have been historically underserved.

Writing
Communication

My lab work significantly strengthened my writing skills, as I was responsible for documenting my code, summarizing data findings, and contributing written content for presentations and reports. I learned to write clear, concise descriptions of complex analytical processes so that other lab members could replicate my work. For example, I created documentation for the R functions I developed, explaining each step of the data processing pipeline in plain language. The dimension I grew in most was proficiency — I developed the technical skill of writing for an academic and research audience, which requires precision, clarity, and adherence to disciplinary conventions. I learned that good research writing is about making complex ideas accessible without oversimplifying them. One area for improvement is developing greater confidence in writing for broader audiences, such as translating findings into blog posts or policy briefs. I plan to continue refining my academic writing as I prepare graduate school applications and eventually publish my own research in peer-reviewed journals.

Organization
Strategic Planning

Managing multiple data files, coding scripts, and research tasks simultaneously required me to develop strong organizational skills. I created systematic file-naming conventions, maintained a detailed log of changes made to datasets, and organized our shared drive so that all lab members could easily locate relevant materials. When we encountered a situation where earlier versions of cleaned data had been overwritten, I took the initiative to implement a version-control system for our project files. The dimension I developed most was performance — I consistently put organizational strategies into practice rather than just understanding them in theory. I learned that effective organization in research is not just about personal productivity; it directly impacts the team's ability to work efficiently and maintain data integrity. I still want to improve my long-term project planning, particularly in anticipating potential bottlenecks before they occur. I will carry these organizational habits into graduate school and beyond, where managing complex, multi-phase research projects will be essential to my success.

Artifact

library(tidyverse)
library(tidytext)
library(readxl)

input_dir <- "/cloud/project/GG_Book"
output_dir <- "/cloud/project"

xlsx_files <- list.files(input_dir, pattern = "*.xlsx", full.names = TRUE)

for (file_path in xlsx_files) {
  filename <- basename(file_path)
  filename_no_ext <- tools::file_path_sans_ext(filename)
  number <- str_extract(filename_no_ext, "(?<=GG_)\\d+")
  
  # Process spatial vocabulary from transcripts
  datavyu <- read_excel(file_path)
  parent_questions <- datavyu %>%
    filter(str_detect(utterance, "\\?"))
}
Figure 3: A screenshot of R code I developed to process parent-child speech transcripts in the Language, Cognition and Development Lab. This script extracts spatial vocabulary from raw transcript data, categorizes each instance by type, and generates summary statistics used in our analysis of how parents' spatial language relates to children's early mathematical achievement. This artifact represents the technical skills I built throughout my time in the lab and illustrates how I translated our research questions into systematic, reproducible data analysis procedures.

Lessons Learned

My experience in the Language, Cognition and Development Lab has fundamentally shaped how I approach learning, problem-solving, and collaboration. I discovered that research is a thoughtful, iterative process requiring patience, precision, and openness to revision. The technical skills I gained in R programming and data analysis have translated directly into my statistics coursework, where I now approach assignments with greater confidence. More broadly, this experience solidified my aspiration to pursue graduate studies in cognitive science. I envision applying the leadership competencies I developed — research, analysis, collaboration, and organization — in future roles that bridge academic research and real-world impact, contributing to a more equitable STEM pipeline.

Sherwin Dai

Husky Leadership Certificate · University of Washington · 2026

Psychology · Data Science · Cognitive Development Research
